era_context: The Classical Greek world in the early 4th century BCE, marking the end of the Corinthian War and the establishment of a new framework for Greek interstate relations under Persian hegemony. Rome was consolidating its position in central Italy. The Persian Achaemenid Empire under Artaxerxes II was at the height of its power and influence in the Mediterranean and Near East.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Most of the Mediterranean beyond Greece and Rome

events.title: King's Peace (Peace of Antalcidas) ends the Corinthian War
events.description: The King's Peace, also known as the Peace of Antalcidas, was negotiated by the Persian Great King Artaxerxes II and imposed on the warring Greek city-states. The peace established that all Greek cities would be autonomous and independent, but that the Persian Great King would be the guarantor and enforcer of this arrangement. Sparta, despite its military dominance, accepted Persian mediation to end the costly Corinthian War. The peace effectively subordinated Greek interstate relations to Persian hegemony and marked a shift in the balance of power in the Mediterranean world. The peace would remain the framework for Greek politics until the rise of Macedon under Philip II.
key_figures: Artaxerxes II (Persian Great King, mediator and enforcer)
events.certainty_note: The King's Peace of 387 BCE is one of the best-attested events of the Classical Greek world. Xenophon's near-contemporary account provides detailed information about the negotiations and terms. The date is firmly established.
cross_references: Established Persian hegemony over Greece until the Macedonian conquest
Led to Spartan dominance of Greece in the following decades

events.title: Plato's Academy formally established in Athens
events.description: Plato's Academy was formally established in Athens, likely in 387 BCE (though some sources suggest 388 BCE). The Academy was founded in a gymnasium dedicated to the hero Academus in the suburbs of Athens. It became one of the most influential intellectual institutions of the ancient world, serving as a center for philosophical inquiry, mathematical study, and education for nearly 900 years. The Academy attracted students from across the Greek world and shaped the development of Western philosophy, mathematics, and science.
key_figures: Xenocrates (early student and later head of the Academy)

sources.name: Diogenes Laërtius, Lives and Opinions of Eminent Philosophers, Book 3

events.certainty_note: The founding of Plato's Academy is traditionally dated to 387 BCE, but the exact date is not definitively established. Diogenes Laërtius, writing in the 3rd century CE, is the primary source for biographical information. The Academy's existence and influence are well-attested in later sources.
cross_references: Part of the intellectual flowering of Classical Athens
Led to the development of Western philosophy and science
Aristotle joined the Academy in 367 BCE

events.title: Roman consolidation in Latium and expansion into central Italy
events.description: Rome continued its consolidation of power in Latium and gradual expansion into central Italy. Following the Gallic sack of 390 BCE and the subsequent period of recovery, Rome was now establishing itself as a major power in the Italian peninsula. Specific military campaigns in 387 BCE are poorly documented, but the general trajectory of Roman expansion is clear from later sources. Rome was subjugating or allying with neighboring Italic tribes and city-states.

sources.name: Livy, History of Rome (Ab urbe condita)

events.certainty_note: The general context of Roman expansion is clear, but specific events in 387 BCE are not well-documented. Livy's account is written centuries after the fact and may conflate or misdate events.
cross_references: Part of Rome's gradual expansion in the 4th century BCE
Followed the Gallic sack of 390 BCE and Roman recovery

events.title: Persian consolidation of control in Egypt and the eastern Mediterranean
events.description: Following the reassertion of Persian control over Egypt in the early 390s BCE, Artaxerxes II consolidated Persian hegemony over Egypt and the eastern Mediterranean. The King's Peace of 387 BCE further solidified Persian influence over Greek affairs. Egypt remained under Persian rule, administered through Persian governors and local Egyptian officials. The Persian Achaemenid Empire was at the height of its territorial extent and influence.

sources.name: Egyptian demotic papyri and administrative records

events.certainty_note: Persian control over Egypt and the eastern Mediterranean is attested in Egyptian administrative records and Greek sources. The consolidation of this control is part of the broader context of Artaxerxes II's reign.
cross_references: Part of Artaxerxes II's reign (404–358 BCE)
Related to the King's Peace of 387 BCE

disconfirming_evidence: The King's Peace of 387 BCE is well-attested and its significance is not seriously disputed. However, some modern scholars have questioned the extent to which the peace actually established Persian hegemony over Greece, arguing that Persian influence was more limited and that Greek city-states retained more autonomy than the traditional interpretation suggests. Additionally, the founding date of Plato's Academy remains uncertain; while 387 BCE is the traditional date, some sources suggest 388 BCE, and the distinction may be artificial. The sparse documentation for Roman activities in 387 BCE reflects the broader historiographic problem of unreliable sources for 4th-century BCE Rome.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 387 BCE is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Xenophon's Hellenica, which provides a near-contemporary and detailed account of the King's Peace. This is one of the best-documented years of the Classical Greek world. The founding of Plato's Academy is attested in later biographical sources and internal evidence from Plato's dialogues. Roman history remains poorly documented and dependent on Livy's much later account. Persian sources are minimal, though Egyptian administrative records provide some evidence of Persian control. The record is heavily weighted toward Greece and Rome; other regions are absent or minimally represented.

graph_edges.note: The King's Peace ended the Corinthian War that began in 395 BCE
The King's Peace established a framework for Greek politics that lasted until the Battle of Mantinea in 362 BCE
Plato's Academy continued to develop and attract students, including Aristotle in 367 BCE
